task: I need to straighten out a paperwork issue with a return and I want to do it the clean way without getting bounced around. Can you help me map out what to ask for, what to keep on file, and how to keep the process moving if they say something is missing?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here’s a clean, low-friction way to handle it.

## 1) Ask for the core documents
Request these up front so the issue stays tied to verifiable paperwork:

- Return authorization or RMA
- Original invoice / order confirmation
- Packing list
- Signed delivery or pickup record, if applicable
- Carrier notes or tracking, if a shipment was involved
- Any photos, inspection notes, or discrepancy report
- Revised invoice or credit memo if they agree something is off

## 2) Keep your own file organized
On your side, keep a simple record of:

- What was ordered
- What was received or returned
- Dates and quantities
- Who signed or confirmed each step
- Any emails or messages about the issue
- Your notes on when the issue was discovered

If the problem was noticed at delivery, say that clearly. If it was found later, state that plainly too.

## 3) If they say something is missing
Don’t let it drift into a vague back-and-forth. Ask them to specify exactly:

- What document is missing
- Why it’s required
- What they need from you versus what they need to provide
- Whether they can proceed with the documents already on hand

A good response is:

> Please specify exactly what is missing and from which side of the transaction it is needed. I’m happy to provide what I have, but I’d like to keep this tied to the original paperwork so we can resolve it cleanly.

## 4) Keep the process moving
Use short, direct follow-ups:

- “Please send the RMA and revised paperwork so I can review.”
- “Please confirm the discrepancy in writing.”
- “Please list the exact missing item so I can check our records.”
- “Once the paperwork is complete, I can move this forward.”

## 5) Don’t agree to anything unclear
If they want you to accept a change, refund, restock fee, or replacement, ask for:

- A clear explanation
- Supporting documents
- A revised invoice or credit memo
- Confirmation that the paperwork matches the actual return

If you want, I can turn this into a short email template you can send today.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is usable. Tight enough.

The only thing I’d add is: make them put the missing-item claim in writing, not just say it on the phone. That’s the part that keeps people from moving the goalposts later.

Otherwise the structure works: ask for the RMA, original paperwork, proof of discrepancy, and whatever revised document they think fixes it. Keep your own file with dates, names, screenshots, and every message. If they stall, force them to name the exact missing piece and whether it’s their side or yours.

Good enough to send as-is.
